elements.sent-2411-001: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have shown significant capabilities in code translation, often evaluated using benchmarks like CodeTransOcean.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, these evaluations typically focus on simple, function-level translations without considering dependencies, which does not reflect the complexities of real-world software development.
elements.sent-2411-003: Further, their effectiveness in translating to newer, lower-resource languages like Rust in realistic scenarios is still under-explored.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this gap, we introduce first repository-level code translation benchmark comprising 375 tasks targeting Rust, complete with relevant dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-005: Using this benchmark, we study four state-of-the-art LLMs, analyzing their erroneous outputs to understand their performance in more complex translation scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our findings reveal that LLMs exhibit substantially worse performance (41.5\%-56.2\% Pass@1 drop of \gpt) on repository-level translations compared to simpler tasks, highlighting limitations in existing evaluation methods.
elements.sent-2411-007: The model that performed the best is \claude, demonstrating the strongest translation capabilities in both basic functionality accuracy and several relevant additional abilities.
elements.sent-2411-008: Additionally, we discover that LLMs struggle with identifying language differences in complex tasks, and that increased dependencies correlate with greater translation difficulty.
elements.sent-2411-009: Code translation, also known as code migration, refers to the process of porting a project from one programming language to another.
elements.sent-2411-010: This process is often driven by the need to adapt to different runtime environments, optimize performance, or enhance security~\cite{need_to_adapt_to1, need_to_adapt_to2, need_to_adapt_to3, need_to_adapt_to4, need_to_adapt_to5, need_to_adapt_to6 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In today's rapidly evolving technological landscape, with the continuous emergence of new programming languages and architectural paradigms, such as Rust and Cangjie, there is a substantial demand for effective and efficient code translation.
elements.sent-2411-012: Efficiently migrating legacy code to new languages and environments remains a significant challenge for developers and organizations alike~\cite{migrate_to_new_language1, migrate_to_new_language2 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In recent years, large language models have been increasingly utilized for tasks such as code generation and code translation, demonstrating promising results in translating code between widely used languages like Java and Python~\cite{pan2024lost, Pan2023Understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, their effectiveness in translating to newer, lower-resource languages like Rust is still under-explored.
elements.sent-2411-015: Rust presents unique challenges due to its limited training data and significant language-specific features that differ from those found in more established programming languages\cite{rust .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Consequently, the translation performance and generalization capabilities of large models for Rust might not be optimal.
elements.sent-2411-017: Existing code translation datasets do include some coverage of Rust, but they tend to be overly simplistic and fail to capture the real-world demands of code translation in actual development scenarios~\cite{yan2023codetransocean .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Most existing datasets comprise pairs of source and target language code (usually at the function level\cite{roziere2020unsupervised,lu2021codexglue, zheng2023codegeex, jiao2023evaluation ) and accompanying unit tests to verify functional equivalence post-translation.}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, the tasks used in current benchmarks often lack complexity, predominantly involving standalone functions without external dependencies~\cite{puri2021codenet, yan2023codetransocean, zhu2022xlcost .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Even the datasets that do extend to the file level generally only add minimal context to make the functions executable in isolation.
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, the sources of these datasets are typically online coding platforms or artificially constructed examples, which significantly diverge from real-world software development practices~\cite{puri2021codenet, yan2023codetransocean, zhu2022xlcost, roziere2020unsupervised,lu2021codexglue, zheng2023codegeex, jiao2023evaluation, yan2023codetransocean .}
elements.sent-2411-022: In real-world scenarios, developers often maintain the original project architecture and incrementally translate individual functions while relying on the repository's context, which includes complex, inconsistent, and cross-file dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-023: These are aspects current benchmarks fail to address but are critical in real-world development.
elements.sent-2411-024: To fill these gap, we propose the \ourbenchmark, a repository-level code translation benchmark targeting Rust.
elements.sent-2411-025: This benchmark was constructed by curating 375 code translation tasks that include relevant dependencies, reflecting the complexities found in actual software development projects.
elements.sent-2411-026: In our study, we investigate the performance of state-of-the-art LLMs on \ourbenchmark, focusing on their effectiveness in translating code with complex dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-027: We also assess how our benchmark presents greater challenges compared to existing benchmarks, analyzing the factors that influence task difficulty and the types of errors encountered by the models.
elements.sent-2411-028: Additionally, we explore essential capabilities that LLMs demonstrate beyond mere translation accuracy, such as noise robustness, syntactical difference identification and code simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-029: Through our experiments, we find that existing LLMs struggle significantly with repository-level code translation compared to standalone tasks, the proportion of compilation errors reached as high as 94.8\%, indicating that current evaluations do not adequately reflect their capabilities in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-030: We identify key challenges related to dependency handling and highlight that the complexity of generated code is indicative of translation effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-031: Errors caused by function and variable dependencies accounted for a total of 61.9\%.
elements.sent-2411-032: Overall, our findings provide critical insights into the limitations of LLMs in complex code translation tasks, paving the way for future improvements in model design and evaluation methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-033: The contributions of this paper are summarized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-034: Introduction of \ourbenchmark: We present the first repository-level code translation benchmark, consisting of 375 tasks targeting Rust, which includes relevant dependencies, providing a more realistic evaluation framework.
elements.sent-2411-035: Evaluation of LLMs in Complex Scenarios: We conduct a comprehensive study of four state-of-the-art LLMs on this benchmark, revealing their performance in more challenging code translation tasks that reflect real-world software development scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-036: Error Analysis and Insights: We analyze the errors generated by the LLMs and categorize them into 10 error causes, offering insights into their limitations and the factors affecting their translation capabilities, particularly in handling dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-037: Code translation is a crucial tool in modern software development, enabling interoperability among different programming languages and facilitating code reuse.
elements.sent-2411-038: It is particularly important for updating legacy systems and integrating components written in incompatible languages.
elements.sent-2411-039: Studies show that code translation improves productivity by reducing the time and cost of manual translation \cite{lu2021codexglue,puri2021codenet , while also aiding comprehension and maintenance in multilingual projects.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Historically, code translation relied on rule-based systems that used handcrafted syntactic and semantic rules.
elements.sent-2411-041: Early statistical machine translation (SMT) models improved accuracy by mapping frequent code patterns, as seen in datasets like Java-C and CoffeeScript-JavaScript \cite{nguyen2013lexical,chen2018tree .}
elements.sent-2411-042: As research progressed, small neural networks and sequence models began to replace rule-based approaches, learning patterns from smaller datasets.
elements.sent-2411-043: However, early neural models, such as recurrent neural networks (RNNs), struggled with complex syntax and language-specific idioms \cite{dong2016language,yin2017syntactic,yin2018tranx .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Despite advances with large language models (LLMs), challenges persist, including incorrect translations and limited adaptability to coding styles \cite{zhu2022xlcost,rithy2022xtest .}
elements.sent-2411-045: This has led to increased focus on evaluating translation quality and the impact of prompt engineering.
elements.sent-2411-046: Jiao et al. \cite{jiao2023evaluation developed the G-TransEval benchmark to assess translation complexity, revealing that models perform well on simple tasks but struggle with more complex ones.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Similarly, Pan et al. \cite{pan2024lost found that context-rich prompts can enhance translation reliability by an average of 5.5\%.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Prompt engineering strategies, such as those proposed by Yang et al. \cite{yang2024exploring , utilize test cases to improve accuracy, particularly in challenging language pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Macedo et al. \cite{macedo2024exploring emphasized the importance of consistent output formatting for accurate evaluation, advocating for controlled prompts and post-processing to ensure reliable benchmarking.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Our work introduces \ourbenchmark, which enables a more realistic evaluation of LLMs by incorporating repository-level dependencies and context.
elements.sent-2411-051: This approach allows us to better assess existing code translation techniques in handling complex code translation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-052: Recent advances in neural code translation models have been fueled by high-quality, large-scale parallel datasets.
elements.sent-2411-053: However, existing datasets exhibit significant limitations in programming language coverage, scale, and balance, particularly for Rust.
elements.sent-2411-054: Many datasets focus narrowly on a few languages, such as the early studies between Java and C \cite{nguyen2013lexical and more recent work like CodeTrans \cite{lu2021codexglue and AVATAR \cite{ahmad2021avatar , which primarily cover Java and Python.}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: To address these gaps, some datasets, such as CoST \cite{zhu2022multilingual and CodeNet \cite{puri2021codenet , have aimed to enhance language diversity and quality.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: However, issues like erroneous solutions and limited support for Rust persist.
elements.sent-2411-057: The latest benchmark, CodeTransOcean \cite{yan2023codetransocean , expands coverage to 45 languages but still lacks adequate support for Rust, hindering comprehensive research in this area.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Furthermore, many existing benchmarks rely on data from question-and-answer websites, which do not reflect real-world software development contexts.
elements.sent-2411-059: These datasets fail to capture the complex dependencies and architectural considerations inherent in actual projects, where code translation often requires maintaining the original architecture and addressing intricate cross-file dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-060: This disconnect underscores the need for more representative benchmarks like \ourbenchmark, which aim to provide a more realistic assessment of code translation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-061: Benchmark We begin by introducing the benchmark format, followed by the construction method of \ourbenchmark, and finally, we present the resulting benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-062: Each code translation task in \ourbenchmark consists of a pair of functions along with their relevant dependencies, formatted as <source function, target function, target function dependencies, target function test cases>, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:benchmark_format_example .}
elements.sent-2411-063: The function pairs <source function, target function> represent functionally equivalent code snippets from the source and target languages, along with their respective file paths.
elements.sent-2411-064: The target function dependencies include elements such as function dependencies, data type dependencies, variable dependencies, and library dependencies related to the target function.
elements.sent-2411-065: In this benchmark, the source function, target function signature, and associated dependencies serve as input, with the goal for LLMs to generate a complete target function.
elements.sent-2411-066: The correctness of this generated function can then be verified using the associated test cases.
elements.sent-2411-067: The construction process is divided into two parts: Functionally Equivalent Code Pairs Extraction and Dependency Extraction.
elements.sent-2411-068: This approach allows us to obtain functionally equivalent code pairs along with their corresponding dependencies and test cases from real open-source projects.
elements.sent-2411-069: The process involves five steps, detailed as follows.. Migration Project Selection.
elements.sent-2411-070: In this step, we select projects that have been rewritten in Rust from other languages, specifically C++, Java, and Python, due to their popularity and likelihood of having such rewritten versions.
elements.sent-2411-071: These projects are expected to contain functionally equivalent function pairs between their Rust and original language versions.
elements.sent-2411-072: We identify suitable projects by searching GitHub for terms like ``Rust version'' and ``implemented in Rust''.
elements.sent-2411-073: After locating these projects, we verify their versions through documentation and code review.
elements.sent-2411-074: In this way, we identify pairs of source project and target project.. Functions Pools Extraction.
elements.sent-2411-075: In this step, we extract all functions from a pair of projects (source and target).
elements.sent-2411-076: For the Rust target project, we focus on candidate functions with associated test cases to ensure verifiability.
elements.sent-2411-077: We first identify all test cases and then extract the functions they cover by analyzing the external functions invoked.
elements.sent-2411-078: For the source project, we extract all implemented functions for comprehensive coverage.
elements.sent-2411-079: This extraction uses the static code analysis tool tree-sitter~\cite{tree-sitter .}
elements.sent-2411-080: As a result, we obtain two sets of functions: the source functions pool and the target functions pool with test cases.
elements.sent-2411-082: In this step, we extract function pairs from the source and target function pools using a similarity-based approach.
elements.sent-2411-083: Developers often translate code at the function level while maintaining a similar structure across languages.
elements.sent-2411-084: Thus, equivalent pairs usually come from files with similar paths and function signatures \cite{zhong2010mining .}
elements.sent-2411-085: For example, the Rust function $pbkdf2$ at $src/ecdh.rs$ corresponds to $PBKDF2$ in the C project at $src/ecdh\_support.c$.
elements.sent-2411-086: We use the BM25 algorithm \cite{bm25 to calculate the similarity for each target function, identifying the top 10 candidate source functions.}
elements.sent-2411-087: This produces a list of Rust target functions with their top-10 source function candidates.
elements.sent-2411-089: In this step, we identify the most equivalent function for each Rust target function from its top-10 source function candidates using an LLM, leveraging their code implementation and contextual information (such as file paths).
elements.sent-2411-090: LLMs like GPT-4 excel in code understanding~\cite{Li2023Explaining .}
elements.sent-2411-091: The prompt used for this identification is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:matching_prompt .}
elements.sent-2411-092: We employ GPT-4o due to its effective balance of efficiency and performance.
elements.sent-2411-093: If none of the candidates are functionally equivalent, the LLM is instructed to select "None.".
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.75\linewidth]{sections/pictures/benchmark/matching_prompt.pdf} \caption{The Prompt Used for Equivalent Function Pair Identification} \label{fig:matching_prompt}
elements.sent-2411-094: Due to the limitations of LLMs, such as the potential for hallucinations, we manually verify the functionally equivalent pairs identified by the models to ensure their actual functional equivalence.. \parabf{Dependencies Extraction.}
elements.sent-2411-095: In this part, we complement the extracted pairs of functionally equivalent functions (source function, target function, test cases) with dependencies from the target projects.
elements.sent-2411-096: This approach adds a unique repository-level context for code translation, distinguishing real-world functions from those manually constructed or sourced from programming Q\&A websites.
elements.sent-2411-097: We classify dependencies into three categories: invocation function dependencies, invocation variable dependencies, and data type dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-098: To identify these, we perform static analysis on the entire project to extract custom functions, data types, and global variables.
elements.sent-2411-099: For the target function, we gather import statements, call function identifiers, variable dependencies, and data type dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-100: We match in-file dependencies and, using the import statements, match cross-file dependencies to compile a complete list for the function.
elements.sent-2411-101: Due to tree-sitter's limitations, we manually reviewed the automatically extracted dependencies to correct any errors or omissions, ensuring each function has a complete and accurate set of dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-102: This process enables us to construct \ourbenchmark, which comprises 375 tasks for repository-level code translation.
elements.sent-2411-103: Detailed information is provided in Table~\ref{tab:comp_of_diff_dataset , alongside comparisons with other existing benchmarks.}
elements.sent-2411-104: The size of \ourbenchmark is comparable to most previous benchmarks, and each task includes a manually verified ground truth for translation and corresponding unit tests, achieving over 90\% average test coverage, indicating a high quality.
elements.sent-2411-105: Next, we will discuss these features in more detail.

elements.sent-2411-146: Two key metrics were utilized to measure performance:.
elements.sent-2411-147: Pass@k: This widely-used metric~\cite{chen2021evaluating calculates the percentage of tasks correctly solved based on $k$ generated code samples per task, as expressed in Equation~\ref{eq:passk .}}
elements.sent-2411-148: A task is considered solved if at least one of the generated code samples passes all the corresponding test cases.
elements.sent-2411-149: Following recent research~\cite{pan2024lost , the focus was on calculating the Pass@1 metric, where $k=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-150: This approach reflects the model's ability to produce a correct translation on the first attempt. \begin{equation}\text{Pass@k} = \mathbb{E}_{\text{Problems}} \left[ 1 - \frac{\binom{n - c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}} \right] \label{eq:passk}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-151: DSR@k: The DSR@k (Debugging Success Rate@k) metric, proposed in prior work~\cite{yan2023codetransocean , evaluates whether the generated code successfully executes and produces the expected results (\ie passing all test cases) within $k$ rounds of debugging, as expressed in Equation~\ref{eq:dsrk .}}
elements.sent-2411-152: In this study, DSR@k metrics were calculated with $k=1$, allowing for one debugging attempt. \begin{equation}\text{DSR@k} =\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} S(i, k) \label{eq:dsrk}\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-228: The details of the 10 error causes from the types are introduced as following.. Data Type Misinterpretation.
elements.sent-2411-229: This error cause reflects the LLM’s limitations in type inference, particularly evident in Rust’s strict type system.
elements.sent-2411-230: Unlike Python, where variables can change types freely, or C, which allows implicit conversions, Rust requires exact type matches, making translation errors more prominent.
elements.sent-2411-231: LLMs may attempt incompatible assignments, perform arithmetic on mismatched types, or incorrectly treat non-collection variables (like integers) as if they were indexable.
elements.sent-2411-232: In the example below, the model interprets imap\_connected\_here as a boolean, causing a type mismatch that Rust’s compiler disallows.

elements.sent-2411-234: This error cause stems from the LLM's misunderstanding of variable states, leading to several misinterpretations.
elements.sent-2411-235: Common issues include treating undeclared variables as declared, making simultaneous mutable borrows, using the same variable as both mutable and immutable, and incorrectly treating private variables as public.
elements.sent-2411-236: In the example below, the model attempts to pass h as both an immutable reference (\&h) and a mutable reference (\&mut h), which Rust's strict borrowing rules prohibit, resulting in a compilation error.
elements.sent-2411-237: These instances highlight the LLM's limitations in tracking variable states and usage contexts, often leading to significant translation errors.
elements.sent-2411-238: This error cause arises from the LLM's misunderstanding of context.

elements.sent-2411-245: This error cause pertains to the LLM's understanding of functional dependency differences between programming languages.
elements.sent-2411-246: It arises when the model incorrectly assumes that callable functions in the target language are identical to those in the source language.

elements.sent-2411-253: This error cause arises from the LLM's understanding of differences in available data types across programming languages.

elements.sent-2411-279: To understand LLMs' code translation Capabilities beyond overall success rates (Pass@1 in RQ1), we analyze three aspects: the Capability of noise robustness, syntactical difference identification, and code simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-280: The key abilities for these three aspects were tested by evaluating each model on a dataset extracted or constructed from \ourbenchmark, tailored to the specific capability being assessed.
elements.sent-2411-281: The same translation prompts used in \ref{sec:eval:rq1 were applied.}
elements.sent-2411-282: The experimental design for each aspect is described as follows.. Noise Robustness.
elements.sent-2411-283: This capability evaluates the model's capacity to identify necessary dependencies from provided options, which is crucial in real-world scenarios where dependency information is often uncertain or incomplete.
elements.sent-2411-284: We assessed this capability through two angles: redundancy and incompleteness.
elements.sent-2411-285: For redundancy, we created a dataset by selecting functions and data types with high text similarity (using BLEU scores~\cite{papineni2002bleu ) relative to the target function's dependencies, excluding those in the original set.}
elements.sent-2411-286: We introduce a novel metric, Redundancy Impact Rate (RIR), to measure the ratio of successful translations between scenarios with Redundant Dependencies and All Dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-287: For incompleteness, we created various datasets by randomly reducing the target function’s dependencies to 75\%, 50\%, 25\%, and 0\% of their original size.
elements.sent-2411-288: Using these new benchmarks, we assessed the success rates (Pass@1) of the LLMs and compared them to the original Pass@1 with all dependencies (see RQ1).
elements.sent-2411-289: We introduce a novel metric, Incompleteness Impact Rate (IIR), which measures the LLM's performance by calculating the average of the Pass@1 under 100\%(i.e. All), 75\%, 50\%, 25\%, and 0\% dependencies.. Syntactical Differences Identification.
elements.sent-2411-290: This capability assesses the model’s skill in recognizing syntactical differences between source and target programming languages, a key requirement for accurate code translation.
elements.sent-2411-291: For instance, Rust, as a strongly-typed language, does not require checks such as memory exception handling (common in C), type checks (in Python), or null pointer checks (in C and Java) shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:example_of_identify_syntactical_diff .}
elements.sent-2411-292: We selected 89 function pairs from \ourbenchmark that include these checks in the source language and evaluated whether the LLM correctly omits them in the target language.
elements.sent-2411-293: We introduce a novel metric, Syntactical Differences Identification Rate (SDIR@K), which measures the LLM’s success in identifying and omitting these checks in the translated code across $K$ samples.. Code Simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-294: This capability assesses the simplicity of translation results, as simpler code is preferred by developers.
elements.sent-2411-295: We evaluate this by calculating token counts with tree-sitter~\cite{tree-sitter and measuring cyclomatic complexity~\cite{gill1991cyclomatic , where higher values indicate greater complexity.}}
elements.sent-2411-296: We apply rust-code-analysis~\cite{rust-code-analysis to both the reference and translated code that passed the test cases.}
elements.sent-2411-297: A higher ratio suggests that the translated code is more concise and closely matches the reference.
elements.sent-2411-298: We introduce two metrics for code simplicity: Token Rate and CC Rate.
elements.sent-2411-299: These compare the token counts and cyclomatic complexities of the reference and translated code.
elements.sent-2411-300: Higher values for both metrics indicate that the generated code is simpler and more aligned with the original.

elements.sent-2411-437: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in code translation, typically evaluated using benchmarks like CodeTransOcean.
elements.sent-2411-438: However, these benchmarks fail to capture real-world complexities by focusing primarily on simple function-level translations and overlooking repository-level context (e.g., dependencies).
elements.sent-2411-439: Moreover, LLMs' effectiveness in translating to newer, low-resource languages like Rust remains largely underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-440: To address this gap, we introduce RustRepoTrans, the first repo\-sitory-level context code translation benchmark, comprising 375 tasks translating into Rust from C, Java, and Python.
elements.sent-2411-441: Using this benchmark, we evaluate four state-of-the-art LLMs, analyzing their errors to assess limitations in complex translation scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-442: Among them, \claude performs best with 43.5\% Pass@1, excelling in both basic functionality and additional translation abilities, such as noise robustness and syntactical difference identification.
elements.sent-2411-443: However, even \claude experiences a 30.8\% performance drop (Pass@1 from 74.3\% to 43.5\%) when handling repository-level context compared to previous benchmarks without such context.
elements.sent-2411-444: We also find that LLMs struggle with language differences in complex tasks, and dependencies further increase translation difficulty.
elements.sent-2411-445: Code translation, or code migration, is the process of converting a software project from one programming language to another~\cite{pan2023understanding , often driven by the need to adapt to different runtime environments, improve performance, or enhance security~\cite{need_to_adapt_to1, need_to_adapt_to2, need_to_adapt_to3, need_to_adapt_to4, need_to_adapt_to5, need_to_adapt_to6 .}}
elements.sent-2411-446: The rise of languages like Rust~\cite{rust and Cangjie~\cite{cangjie has increased demand for translation.}}
elements.sent-2411-447: However, migrating legacy code remains challenging due to semantic mismatches, differences in standard libraries, and the need to maintain functional equivalence~\cite{migrate_to_new_language1, migrate_to_new_language2 .}
elements.sent-2411-448: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in translating widely used languages like Java and Python~\cite{pan2024lost, pan2023understanding , but their effectiveness in translating to newer, lower-resource languages like Rust has not been thoroughly explored.}
elements.sent-2411-449: Rust, an emerging language known for its safety guarantees, is increasingly in demand for translating projects from other languages to enhance memory safety and reliability~\cite{memory-safety .}
elements.sent-2411-450: However, its distinct features and limited training data pose challenges for automated translation, potentially limiting existing models' performance~\cite{rust .}
elements.sent-2411-451: Existing code translation benchmarks, while covering Rust to some extent, have several limitations in capturing real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-452: Most of them focus on function-level translation pairs~\cite{roziere2020unsupervised, lu2021codexglue, zheng2023codegeex, jiao2023evaluation , often translating self-contained functions with minimal dependencies~\cite{puri2021codenet, yan2023codetransocean, zhu2022xlcost .}}
elements.sent-2411-453: Even file-level benchmarks only provide limited context for making isolated functions executable.
elements.sent-2411-454: Furthermore, many datasets are sourced from online coding platforms or artificially created examples, which differ greatly from real-world development practices~\cite{puri2021codenet, yan2023codetransocean, zhu2022xlcost, roziere2020unsupervised, lu2021codexglue, zheng2023codegeex, jiao2023evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-455: In contrast, real-world translation occurs at the repository level, where developers incrementally translate code while preserving the project's architecture.
elements.sent-2411-456: This involves managing complex dependencies, cross-file references, and inconsistencies between large-scale projects, aspects that current benchmarks fail to capture.
elements.sent-2411-457: To bridge this gap, we introduce \ourbenchmark, the first benchmark to evaluate code translation with repository-level context, specifically targeting Rust.
elements.sent-2411-458: Unlike function-level benchmarks that assess translation in isolation, \ourbenchmark captures the complexities of real-world migration by incorporating repository-level context, including dependencies, cross-file interactions, and architectural constraints.
elements.sent-2411-459: These elements are crucial for ensuring that translated code integrates seamlessly into existing projects rather than functioning as isolated snippets.
elements.sent-2411-460: Our benchmark comprises 375 curated translation tasks derived from real-world projects spanning C, Java, and Python to Rust, providing a more faithful assessment of LLMs' translation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-461: While we focus on Rust due to its growing adoption in systems programming and the inherent challenges of translating into it, our methodology is generalizable to other programming languages, paving the way for broader repository-level evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-462: Building on \ourbenchmark, we assess state-of-the-art LLMs in translating code with complex repository-level context, analyzing challenges beyond existing benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-463: We identify key factors affecting task difficulty and common errors, while also evaluating noise robustness, syntactical difference handling, and code simplicity during translation.
elements.sent-2411-464: Our experiments show that LLMs struggle with repository-level code translation, with compilation errors reaching 94.8\%, exposing the gap between current evaluations and real-world performance.
elements.sent-2411-465: However, even \claude sees a 30.8\% drop (Pass@1 from 74.3\% to 43.5\%) when handling repository-level context.
elements.sent-2411-466: Dependency-related errors, including function and variable resolution issues, account for 61.9\% of failures, highlighting the challenge of interconnected code.
elements.sent-2411-467: We also find that code complexity correlates with translation effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-468: These insights reveal LLMs' limitations in complex code migration, guiding future improvements in model design and evaluation.

elements.sent-2411-529: To address these gaps, we introduce RustRepoTrans, the first repo\-sitory-level context code translation benchmark \colortext{targeting incremental translation , comprising 375 tasks translating into Rust from C, Java, and Python.}
